mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar F-Pace P400e engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F-Pace P400e engine (PT204 with hybrid system) typically costs £3,000‑£4,500 supply only. Fitting adds £1,200‑£1,800. Most Jaguar engine replacements range from £3,000 to £9,000 depending on the model and engine type. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar F-Pace P400e engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The PT204 is generally a robust engine, but the P400e has known issues. A safety recall (R/2023/088) was issued for the coolant pump drive belt, which can fail and cause rapid overheating and potential fire risk. Other concerns include HV battery degradation, 12V battery issues, turbo oil feed pipe leaks, and timing chain tensioner wear. With regular maintenance and proper battery care, it can be a reliable and powerful powertrain.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar F-Pace P400e?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most serious issue is the coolant pump drive belt recall – if the belt fails, coolant flow is lost, causing rapid overheating and potential fire risk. Other concerns include HV battery degradation (reduced electric range), 12V battery failure, turbo oil feed pipe leaks, and timing chain tensioner wear causing cold‑start rattle.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an F-Pace P400e engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Given the F-Pace's retained value (typically £25,000‑£50,000+), a £3,000‑£5,500 reconditioned engine is a modest fraction of the vehicle's worth. Replacement is almost always financially viable. However, if the HV battery has also failed (costing £2,000‑£5,000+), the total repair cost may be substantial. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my F-Pace's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F-Pace P400e uses the PT204 (AJ20P4) 2.0‑litre turbo petrol engine combined with a 105 kW electric motor. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. The P400e badge itself confirms the powertrain – it's the 404 PS plug‑in hybrid offered in the X761 F-Pace from 2020 onwards.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair an F-Pace P400e engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – coolant pump belt (recall), turbo oil feed pipe leaks, timing chain wear (if caught early) – are repairable. HV battery degradation may be covered under warranty if the vehicle is still within the warranty period. However, if the engine suffers major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar F-Pace P400e Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs; warranty claims; full provenance

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the F-Pace P400e generation. As a PHEV, the hybrid system components (electric motor, battery, inverter) add complexity and cost to any replacement. Most Jaguar engine replacements range from £3,000 to £9,000 depending on the model and engine type.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an F-Pace P400e engine typically takes 12‑16 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Jaguar specialist). Hybrid system integration adds to the complexity.

problems.title: Coolant Pump Drive Belt Failure — Safety Recall (R/2023/088)
problems.symptoms: Engine overheating warning, coolant loss, steam from under bonnet, potential fire risk — may occur without prior warning. A concern has been identified on Ingenium I4 2.0L Petrol PHEV engines where the coolant pump drive belt can become damaged leading to detachment or destruction.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — recall affects vehicles built from 19 December 2019 to 6 February 2023.
problems.repairCost: £0 (if recalled — dealer repair free) / £500–£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (if out of warranty).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored overheating can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see overheating or steam, stop driving immediately.

problems.recommendation: A safety recall (R/2023/088) has been issued for Ingenium I4 2.0L Petrol PHEV engines where the coolant pump drive belt can become damaged leading to detachment. Where coolant pump drive is lost, there will be a loss of coolant flow, causing the engine to rapidly overheat and subsequently rupture an engine‑mounted plastic elbow in the cooling circuit. Coolant can leak onto the turbocharger heatshield which may lead to a fire in the engine compartment. Check your VIN against the recall database immediately. If you see any red warning lamps or signs of smoke, stop the vehicle and move away.

problems.title: HV Battery Degradation / 12V Battery Issues
problems.symptoms: Reduced electric‑only range (reported drop from claimed 33 miles to 27 miles or less), hybrid system not working, vehicle running only on petrol, 12V battery starting issues, dashboard warnings.
problems.typicalMileage: 20,000‑50,000 miles (age‑related as much as mileage — the 17.1 kWh battery is subject to degradation over time).
problems.repairCost: £2,000‑£5,000+ [PLACEHOLDER] (HV battery replacement — if available) / £200–£500 (12V battery replacement).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — battery replacement only (if ignored, the vehicle runs as a conventional petrol only).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if HV battery warnings appear or if electric range drops significantly.

problems.recommendation: HV battery failure is a significant concern on the F-Pace P400e. Owners have reported reduced electric range — fully charged indicating 27 miles rather than the claimed 33 miles. The 12V battery is also a known issue, with some owners experiencing starting problems on 3‑year‑old vehicles. If your vehicle displays HV battery warnings or starting issues, have it diagnosed promptly. Check whether your vehicle is still under warranty — battery replacement can be expensive.

problems.title: Turbo Oil Feed Pipe Leak (PT204)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, sudden loss of power, hesitation, limp mode.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to turbo failure).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected or power loss occurs.

problems.recommendation: Some owners have reported turbo oil drain tube leaks on the F-Pace P400e. One owner experienced sudden loss of power while driving — the car hesitated and acceleration was significantly reduced, almost as if it went into limp mode. The AA found that it was the seal at the oil feed pipe connection to the turbo. If you notice oil spots under your vehicle or experience sudden power loss, have the turbo oil feed system inspected immediately.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (PT204)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine on cold start, engine misfires or rough idling, poor acceleration and reduced power, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: £3,000–£5,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The Ingenium petrol engine can suffer from timing chain tensioner wear. Owners have reported that modified timing chains and guides on newer vehicles have resolved the issue. However, emission issues are common on engines at 50k plus. A rattling noise from the engine compartment may be caused by a Variable Camshaft Timing (VCT) unit internal seal that is below specification. A preventative chain and tensioner replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Jaguar F-Pace P400e — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Recall repair is free; out‑of‑warranty repair still cheaper than engine replacement
May be covered under warranty; if not, assess cost vs. vehicle value
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valve damage is almost certain
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Jaguar F-Pace P400e? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Jaguar service history with correct hybrid system maintenance
* Recall (R/2023/088 — coolant pump belt) verified as completed — this is safety‑critical
* HV battery health check completed and documented
* Warranty remaining — the P400e is a complex, expensive vehicle to repair out of warranty
avoidIf: * No evidence of recall completion — coolant pump belt failure is a safety issue
* Any sign of HV battery warnings or reduced electric range
* No evidence of regular charging — the hybrid battery may have been neglected
* Any sign of timing chain rattle on cold start

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Jaguar F-Pace P400e is a compelling plug‑in hybrid SUV, combining the PT204 2.0‑litre turbo petrol engine with a 105 kW electric motor for 404 PS and 640 Nm. It offers genuine electric‑only capability for short commutes (up to 33 miles) and impressive performance (0‑60 mph in 5.0 seconds). However, PHEV complexity brings potential issues — a safety recall for the coolant pump drive belt, HV battery degradation, 12V battery issues, turbo oil feed pipe leaks, and timing chain tensioner wear. Buy on service history above all else — a vehicle with documented recall completion, battery health checks, and regular maintenance is a safe bet; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare F-Pace P400e engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar F-Pace P400e (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Recall Status — Is recall R/2023/088 (coolant pump belt) completed? This is safety‑critical
2. Service History — Full history with correct hybrid system maintenance
3. HV Battery Health — Has the battery been tested? What's its state of health?
4. Electric Range — Does the displayed range match expectations? (33 miles claimed, real‑world ~20‑27 miles)
5. Cold Start — Any rattling? (timing chain tensioner warning)
6. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle for any signs of oil leakage (turbo oil feed pipe)
7. 12V Battery — Any starting issues? (12V battery is a known weak point)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
